source_alignment.subject: English
source_alignment.source_statement: Acquire a wide vocabulary, understand increasingly challenging texts, and use Standard English confidently in discussion, reading and writing across a range of contexts and purposes.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This Year 7 bridge pack supports the transition to secondary disciplinary language by teaching morphology, context, register and precise word choice across English, science, history, geography and mathematics texts.

objective.child_goal: I can work out, sort and use subject vocabulary accurately in different lessons.
objective.statement: Use morphology, context and disciplinary purpose to infer, sort and apply academic vocabulary across KS3 subject texts.
misconceptions: Chooses a word's everyday meaning when the subject context gives it a more precise disciplinary meaning.
Treats prefixes, roots and suffixes as decoration rather than clues to meaning and word class.
Sorts vocabulary by topic association only, not by how the word is used in the discipline.
Uses impressive-sounding words without checking register, precision or sentence fit.
Assumes one glossary definition is enough without testing meaning in a fresh context.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Words that have precise meanings in a subject, such as evidence in English or energy in science.
vocabulary.audio_script: Disciplinary vocabulary means subject words used precisely in that discipline.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A word card moving between subject doors and changing its example sentence.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The study of meaningful word parts, such as prefixes, roots and suffixes.
vocabulary.audio_script: Morphology helps us break a word into meaningful parts.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A word split into prefix, root and suffix tiles.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The core part of a word that carries its main meaning.
vocabulary.audio_script: The root is the central meaning part of a word.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A root tile supporting a word-family tree.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The words, subject and situation around a word that help us work out meaning.
vocabulary.audio_script: Context gives clues from the sentence, paragraph and subject.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A target word surrounded by clue cards.

vocabulary.child_explanation: How formal, technical or conversational language is for a purpose and audience.
vocabulary.audio_script: Register is the level of language that fits the audience, purpose and subject.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A slider moving from everyday to formal to technical.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort each word card by whether it is used in an everyday way, a technical subject way, or both.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate flexible word meaning and expose the everyday-versus-disciplinary trap.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Some words change meaning depending on the subject and sentence around them.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Word cards move between everyday, technical and both columns with example-sentence previews.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Break the word into prefix, root and suffix, then predict its meaning and word class.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce morphology as a reusable strategy for unfamiliar academic words.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Prefixes, roots and suffixes are clues. They do not replace context, but they give us a strong first prediction.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A word splits into labelled morpheme tiles that feed a meaning prediction card.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: In science, why does 'observe' mean more than just 'look'?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model subject-context reasoning and precise definition building.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: In science, observe means to notice or measure carefully so evidence can be recorded.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The everyday word look is refined into a science glossary card with evidence and record icons.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort each vocabulary card into the subject context where it is being used most precisely.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise using sentence clues and disciplinary purpose rather than topic association alone.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Read the whole sentence. Ask what work the word is doing in this subject.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Sentence cards dock into English, science, history, geography and mathematics boards.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build a visual glossary card with meaning, word parts, subject context and one accurate sentence.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check that learners can consolidate vocabulary knowledge beyond matching a definition.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A useful glossary card shows what the word means, how it is built, where it belongs and how it works in a sentence.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A glossary card fills with definition, morphemes, context icon, example and non-example.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Lyra why using a long word is not the same as using a precise word.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair register and precision misconceptions through explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I chose the longest word because it sounded clever. What should I check before using it?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion compares a vague impressive sentence with a precise subject-fit sentence.

manipulatives.purpose: Make unfamiliar vocabulary inspectable by splitting words into prefix, root and suffix tiles linked to meaning and word class.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners select or type word parts, test meaning predictions against context, build word-family branches, identify word class and repair overconfident morphology guesses.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Tiles support keyboard, switch and tap input; each tile has audio, symbol and simple-text explanations; dyslexia-friendly spacing, no timed pressure and reduced-motion static tile states support SEND learners.

manipulatives.purpose: Show that vocabulary meaning depends on subject purpose, sentence context, register and evidence of use.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners sort word-in-sentence cards into subject contexts, compare everyday and technical meanings, choose precise replacements and justify decisions with clue highlighting.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Linear scan order, large sortable cards, non-colour subject patterns, screen-reader labels, simple text mode and optional spoken sentence replay make the sorter accessible without drag-only control.

manipulatives.purpose: Create reusable glossary cards that connect definition, morphology, context, example, non-example and visual anchor.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners assemble or complete cards, select visual anchors, record or choose an example sentence and revisit cards in spaced retrieval.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Cards can be rendered as plain text, printed or spoken; icons always have text labels; switch users can complete one field at a time.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave morphology with context sorting so learners do not rely on word parts alone; revisit vocabulary from English, science, history, geography and mathematics using short subject-authentic extracts.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use prefixes, roots and suffixes to make a meaning prediction and identify likely word class.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: academic word + morpheme tiles + meaning clues + context sentence + prediction check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use high-utility KS3 word parts such as re-, inter-, sub-, -tion, -ity, graph, struct, port and spect; avoid false certainty where etymology is opaque.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Distinguish everyday and disciplinary meanings using sentence and subject clues.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: polysemous academic word + two or three subject sentences + sort, match and explain prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include words such as evidence, source, table, volume, current, function, character and observe with safe, supplied contexts.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Sort vocabulary by how a discipline uses the word, not merely by a related topic.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: subject boards + sentence cards + clue-highlighting + reason stem
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use short, subject-authentic but self-contained sentences; no item should require prior specialist knowledge beyond the supplied text.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Build visual glossary cards with meaning, word parts, context, example and non-example.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: target word + partial card + icon/definition/example choices + simple text alternative
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Every visual anchor must have text labels and audio; examples should be accurate in the named discipline.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose vocabulary that fits register, meaning and sentence grammar rather than the longest or most dramatic word.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: subject sentence gap + purpose/audience note + four vocabulary choices + register feedback
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid penalising dialect or home language; focus on subject precision and Standard English requirements for school writing.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain vocabulary knowledge through short spaced retrieval across subjects.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: reviewed glossary card + fresh sentence + choose, complete or explain one use
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep retrieval prompts short and support read-aloud, simple text and glossary-card preview.

body.prompt: Use the word parts to predict the meaning of 'reconstruct' in this history sentence: 'Historians reconstruct the events from letters and records.'
choices: build again or piece together again

expected_answer.value: build again or piece together again

hints: The prefix re- often means again.
Use the sentence: historians use letters and records to piece events together.
question_variants.explanation: Reconstruct combines re- meaning again with construct meaning build; in history, it means piecing together events again from evidence.

body.prompt: Match the word 'evidence' to the subject meaning used in each sentence.

cards.sentence: In English, evidence from the text supports an inference about the character.
cards.answer: quotation or detail from a text

cards.sentence: In science, evidence from the experiment supports the conclusion.

choices: quotation or detail from a text

value: English: quotation or detail from a text

hints: Look at the subject and what kind of proof that subject uses.
English often uses text details; science often uses observations or measurements.
question_variants.explanation: Evidence has a shared idea of support, but the precise form changes by subject: textual detail in English and measured observations in science.

body.prompt: Complete a visual glossary card for the geography word 'erosion'.
body.subject: Geography

meaning: the wearing away and movement of rock or soil
the number of people in a group
accurate_sentence: Coastal erosion can change the shape of a cliff.
Erosion is when a character speaks.
Erosion means counting in equal steps.
non_example: a new layer of rock being added
soil being worn away by water

body.simple_text: Build the card using definition, word parts, picture idea, example and non-example.

value.meaning: the wearing away and movement of rock or soil
value.accurate_sentence: Coastal erosion can change the shape of a cliff.
value.non_example: a new layer of rock being added

hints: Choose the meaning that fits geography.
A non-example should help show what the word does not mean.
question_variants.explanation: The completed card connects erosion to wearing away, the related word erode, a coastal visual anchor, a correct geography sentence and a contrasting non-example.

body.prompt: Choose the most precise word for this science sentence: 'The beaker was heated until the liquid began to ____.'

hints: The sentence needs a formal science word.
Choose the word that precisely means changing from liquid to gas.
question_variants.explanation: Evaporate is the precise science vocabulary for a liquid changing into a gas; the other choices are vague or too informal.

body.prompt: Sort the word 'source' by the subject meaning in each sentence.

cards.sentence: The historian checked whether the source was written by an eyewitness.
cards.subject: History

cards.sentence: The river's source is high in the hills.
cards.subject: Geography

cards.sentence: Use the source text to find a quotation for your inference.
cards.subject: English

choices: a text used for reading evidence

value: English: a text used for reading evidence

hints: Read the whole sentence, not just the word.
Ask what job the word is doing in that subject.
question_variants.explanation: Source changes its precise meaning with the discipline: a historical document, the beginning of a river, or a text used as evidence.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Chooses a word's everyday meaning when the subject context gives it a more precise disciplinary meaning.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects an everyday paraphrase that does not fit the subject sentence.
repair_sequence: Read the sentence and name the subject context.
Highlight clue words that show how the discipline uses the term.
Compare the everyday meaning with the technical meaning and choose the one that fits.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains a new polysemous word using subject and sentence clues.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats prefixes, roots and suffixes as decoration rather than clues to meaning and word class.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner ignores morphemes or gives a guess unrelated to the word parts.
repair_sequence: Split the word into visible morpheme tiles.
Attach a provisional meaning to each reliable tile.
Check the prediction against the full context sentence.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner uses morphology and context together to infer a new academic word.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Sorts vocabulary by topic association only, not by how the word is used in the discipline.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner sorts a word into science, history or English because the topic seems related, while the sentence uses a different disciplinary meaning.
repair_sequence: Underline the verb and noun phrase around the target word.
Ask what evidence, action or idea the word controls in the sentence.
Sort by word use and justify with a sentence clue.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner sorts a fresh card by disciplinary use and cites the clue that proves it.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses impressive-sounding words without checking register, precision or sentence fit.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses the longest or most dramatic word even when it is vague, informal or grammatically awkward.
repair_sequence: Name the purpose and audience of the sentence.
Test whether each choice has the exact meaning needed.
Read the complete sentence aloud or with text-to-speech to check grammar and register.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner chooses a precise subject-fit word and rejects a showy but unsuitable option.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Assumes one glossary definition is enough without testing meaning in a fresh context.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner can match a definition but cannot choose an accurate example or non-example.
repair_sequence: Build a glossary card with definition, example and non-example.
Explain why the example fits and the non-example does not.
Apply the word in a new sentence from a different but related context.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner completes a visual glossary card and uses the word accurately in a fresh sentence.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm card movement, matte backgrounds, no countdowns and no flashing correct/incorrect effects.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace tile splitting, docking and register sliders with static labelled panels and step-by-step reveals.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read the target word, morpheme parts, context sentence and answer choices aloud, with replay for glossary cards.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use line focus, adjustable spacing, syllable and morpheme chunking, glossary popovers and short subject-context extracts.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Present one decision at a time: word parts, context clues, subject sort, precision check, then example sentence.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow card rehearsal, spoken planning, morpheme reference cards and extra wait time before sentence-fit answers.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Credit a correct clue or morpheme even when the final meaning needs repair, then offer one focused next step.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Provide visual glossary cards, cognate notes where reliable, spoken models and explicit everyday-versus-technical comparisons.
adaptive_support.switch_access: All sorters and glossary builders have linear scan order, confirm/cancel controls and no drag-only path.
adaptive_support.simple_text: Every visual glossary, morphology and sorter task has a plain text version with the same answer model.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires using morphology and context to infer and sort disciplinary vocabulary; secure requires applying precise vocabulary in fresh subject sentences and explaining why everyday, vague or showy choices do not fit.
evidence.parent_summary: Can work out and use important subject vocabulary by checking word parts, context and precision.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses morphemes and context together, distinguishes everyday from technical meanings, builds transferable glossary cards and selects precise subject-fit vocabulary.
next_actions: Interleave disciplinary vocabulary with argument structure and literature inference.
Connect morphology routines to science, history, geography and mathematics reading tasks.
Use learner glossary cards for spaced retrieval before extended subject writing.

qa.notes: Review-status Year 7 disciplinary vocabulary pack aligned to England KS3 English language, reading, writing and spoken-language aims. SEND design includes morphology builders, discipline-context sorters, visual glossary cards, simple text, audio-first paths and switch access before pilot release.
